speaker: interviewer
text: 12th of April, 1996. John, can you explain this list of names that you have here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I don't really remember every-- every-- every-- every name. I mean, because it's been such a long time. But I mean, it's-- [INAUDIBLE] rabbi, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: He passed away with-- with six other members of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what is the list?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this list? What is the list of who--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the list. The list is for the people who-- who perished in-- [? during the ?] Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: From where?

speaker: interviewee
text: From 1940-- '40-- 1944, March till 1945 May.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and these were all the people from your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. All the people. And I'm-- I'm sure that I knew all of them. But sometimes my memory fades. And some people who I didn't see so often or I didn't have much to do with them. But the more I read, the more it comes back to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and the list that you have here are the people who perished.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There's a list here of people who perished. And there's a list here of people who survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the first page, is all perished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And these Lowys are members of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not all of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: This one, Lowy Hugo, this is our father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lowy Gyula, that is not relation. See, the Lowy name was quite frequent in-- in central Europe. It is quite few other Lowys too. It is not only one family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And-- and the second page?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just [INAUDIBLE]. This is Father's eldest brother, Lowy Lipot and all their children. [INAUDIBLE] Not one of them survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: But any of your father's family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's family, only the two survived. The brother Izsak or [? Izzo, ?] which he passed away now, and father's sister, which is [INAUDIBLE] nanny, we call her. But actually, [? Ziolanda ?], which she's still alive. She lives in London-- two daughters married there. And she would be today 90 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And how many children were there in your father's family?

speaker: interviewee
text: All.

speaker: interviewer
text: All. Just how many were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like my cousins?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. How many-- how many many siblings did your father have?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father? There was four children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Four children in his family. And two of them survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I'm sorry. This, you have to cut this off. Something I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. In your father's family,

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not-- not the [? you-- ?] my father's family.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, not your own. Just your-- and you can [? go into it. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to really think because it's, again, I gave you that on the list. It's-- Father's was nine children alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nine children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And two-- two survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only two survived. And in your mother's family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother's family, there was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: None survived except my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Is there anybody else on the-- on this list that you'd like to mention?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's [? Haupt, ?] you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's husband of my auntie, who is still alive in-- in London. He perished. And after that is-- that means him and his mother-in-law, which is his-- his mother. That's [INAUDIBLE]. She perished too.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this is a continuation of the same list?

speaker: interviewee
text: Continuation, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this list?

speaker: interviewee
text: And these are the survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four to six.

speaker: interviewer
text: Survived out of a total of?

speaker: interviewer
text: Citizens of--

speaker: interviewee
text: 458 Perished.

speaker: interviewer
text: --from Fil'akovo alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we arrived to Cyprus, we have been taken to a camp, which it looked like a concentration camp anywhere. It was barbed wires up to about three-- three meters, or three and a half meter and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] coiled barbed wires between the two. It actually was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm sorry. [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] didn't come to my mind yet. Actually. [LAUGHS] There were two barbed-- barbed wire fences. And between the fences there was this cord barbed wire that makes sure it's very difficult to get through. And every hundred meters there was a watch tower which remained all the time by-- by the army. And there was a sentry, or-- or army working around the camp. Everything's inside. I mean-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which-- which army?

speaker: interviewee
text: The British army. We're talking about the British army. Yes. And then these open trucks, which were-- [INAUDIBLE] the army, and the flood lights. So really, it was done in such a way that it's make it as secure as possible not to escape. And the general setup in the camps were that British army never stepped in-- into the camp. If they came in for some reason, they were unarmed. And we had a committee. So we-- we dealt with our own sort of a management of the camp. Tents and sleeping [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] bags, and anything which [INAUDIBLE], anything [INAUDIBLE] saw sort of a sleeping-- sleeping facilities, and food, and accommodation was also supplied into the-- into the camp. And we were responsible to distribute it in a way, and look after it, and do it. And the same thing with food, was distributed once or twice a week. And we looked after it. We even got some ration of cigarettes, which was 10 or 12 cigarettes a week, which was [INAUDIBLE] very much for the smoker. And as a punishment, the first thing was that they took the cigarettes away from us. We-- when we got to the camp, we didn't know how long and want. So we started to organize ourselves in the best possible way. For that one, there was-- it was a party system. It was really like a governmental sort of an institution. Each party [? so ?] depends how many members they were. [INAUDIBLE] They-- they selected their own representative. And I was selected to represent the [INAUDIBLE], which we belonged to, and also the-- sort of represent any other affiliation with [? some of my ?] movement. This organization worked, I think it's quite efficiently. Because we had to look after our own welfare, keep the spirit of the people up, because we had quite a lot of problem. People were mentally and emotionally damaged. I mean, so it was very close to the time when-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] when after the liberation. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Or, even so, that-- [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: After-- Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: After the liberation-- so people were-- were sort of damaged, mentally. So a lot of people really went around the bend. So we had to do as much as possible to keep sort of the spirit of the people up and make them to this day as useful and as possible. There wasn't much work available because there was nothing to be done. The only work, which was really available was collecting the rubbish, and cleaning toilets, and something of that, which was enumerated with some sort of a money. I think the Joint or some Jewish agents or something, they provided a certain-- certain budget for this so that people are working. They get some money for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there wasn't hardly-- there was not much work available. So we do it in-- in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] shifts. And the money, you ask what's the money for there? What can you buy? They also established a-- a kiosk sense of a shop. It was everything little, toothpaste, toothbrush, some food that was available, so you can convert your money into these [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: But our situation as mine was much better because we always had this group of 20. And we shared really everything. So when you-- when you do it-- but individual [INAUDIBLE] people, they really suffered quite a lot. They suffered emotionally or socially. And we made our life as good as possible we can. Now, there was-- like people everywhere, there were certain things which was [? cause ?] which we were against it. Say, for example, the British started to tease us continuously or influence us or make our decision to give the Zionists idea away. They were throwing sort of leaflets into the camp. They say, anybody who wants for the next two weeks, they want to get out of the camp, we guaranteed to settle down in Canada, Australia, or even in South Africa. And I mean, people are just people. So you have to-- you see, we could not let the spirit down. If you get something like this started, something would be lost. So we kept our own sort of a policing, the whole thing. We had people working-- that the British were working outside the camp. And we were working inside of the camp. Nobody-- so that we should pass any information-- because it was said they put a stone and throw it over. And who did, I mean, we had to sort of punish them in some way. And people were stealing. People also, we put them-- sometimes we put them in jail for two or three months. So I mean this committee, we dealt with every possible problem. And sort of that gave me, personally, a good outlet of-- of doing certain things. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Achieving something.

speaker: interviewee
text: And achieving certain things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's on the photo here?

speaker: interviewee
text: The photographs are all representatives of factions of-- of-- of community. I mean, my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Are all-- are all these people from the 20 that you traveled with from Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It's-- I am the only one from the 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see--

speaker: interviewee
text: The only people-- I think if I remember correctly, that camp 68 had 8,000 or 9,000 people. So this one represented 9,000-- 9,000 people. I'm this one on the top right.

speaker: interviewer
text: The young boy at the back.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the youngest one.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. All of them, I mean, this fellow was representing the religious group, [INAUDIBLE] Israel. This one was representing-- there was sort of a partisan party. He was all partisan, this one.

speaker: interviewer
text: The one in the middle?

speaker: interviewee
text: This one in the middle. Yes. And this was our sort of-- looks after money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Treasurer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Treasurer because we had some money as well. This one was representing the [INAUDIBLE], which is a very left. I mean, I could tell you. They will be all representing groups of-- of people who are--

speaker: interviewer
text: Affiliated with Israeli political parties.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I mean, we all have been. I mean, why-- why would we be there? I mean, nobody was [INAUDIBLE] to the country. So we are-- we are all Zionists. And I think the biggest problem we have always was distributing the cigarettes. Because, I mean, at that time, people smoked quite heavily. And 12 cigarettes a week doesn't go very far for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- amazingly enough, I was distributing the cigarettes. So I had to have a few body guards around me.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] At one time-- some-- some-- new groups were coming, which was in 1967. And it was a lot of protests and demonstration. We achieved from the British that they don't isolate each-- each camp on its own. So they provided an overhead breach that we have communicated. And then we ordered-- so we get to distribute more cigarettes. Two young, strong boys came from my-- from camp 67. They demanded the-- their cigarettes. I mean, I realized. I knew them from 67. And so they threatened me physically. So I just had to get one whistle. And I had about 12 [INAUDIBLE]. So it was quite so  good.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these-- these people represented the 8,000, 9,000 people in camp 68.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in camp 67?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. But we were always--

speaker: interviewer
text: Similar numbers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Always similar life. And so anyhow, Cyprus we had this soccer team. We had really all sorts of-- of-- other activities. But it was too long. I mean, you know, it was times where some people take that idea of rebelling and-- and maybe breaking out.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that would have been completely senseless because what-- what-- how can you do that? And out of this-- so there were-- after a while when things did not move, we started as sort of an action to escape from the camp. And with the help of the-- of the fishing fleet, which was manned by Cyprus, Greece, for good money, they were willing to-- to shift a few people from-- from Cyprus to-- to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: It worked that way that you have to be a volunteer first if you want. And if you volunteered to escape, then you went through very rigor, sort of, exercises, because you have to be able to-- to crawl two kilometers. I mean, by the end-- the end this-- [? this course, ?] you have a limitless distance to crawl, and also, mentally have to prepare. Because as I said, this was all manned by-- by an army with-- with rifles and with bullets. I mean, it was-- but during this sort of days and nights, we-- we took a exact sort of details of movement, a movement of people, of-- of century. I mean, you were more or less, you know, were to sort of escape. Escape was always was at night. And whichever way it is, there is a blind spot, which they can't see from the top. So [INAUDIBLE] worked out. And when we were ready for-- for escape-- so there was a time when the outside contact was-- must have been ready too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we waiting for a moonless night. And one when in front, who was cutting the-- the wires. And the British was a few-- few meters up from us. So we done it in such a way that he didn't have anything for opening, right? We all had to crawl down, and started to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's two people always left behind. It was a group of 12 each time. So when you go there, you get a sort of a [INAUDIBLE]. It's like jumping from an airplane, you know, when-- when you skydive. So another push. And there was something which was a little bit harder to push, them we push them aside. We stay, you know, [INAUDIBLE]. And when we went out, there was a-- a contact point where some of the contact was waiting and taken to a harbor, and put them on a fishing boat, and get them to Israel. I tried twice to escape. And both times, I did not succeed. The first time, because there must have been something not going, in [? accordance ?] of time, either outside or there. So we delayed going out. And also by the time we got outside the camp, and we get to the meeting point, which was part of a rural area, the contact is gone because it's become to be light.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to camouflage ourselves, dig ourselves into the, you know, leaves and-- and dry wood. And so during the day, some kids came to collect dry wood. And obviously they stepped on someone's head. So you know, [INAUDIBLE]. We had to make clear ourselves. It was standard we should not use any force or anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Once they discovered us, we have to give up. So in no time, the British army came and collected us. And we being punished and sort of-- and taken back to the camp again. The second time when I went, I went-- unfortunately, the way it happened that when we walked out, the sentry, they put an additional sentry-- was one, you know, sentry in a few hundred meters, then another sentry. And we just bumped into another sentry.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I will caught the-- the second time. Yes. And then I-- I had a sort of-- they-- they shifted me from camp-- camp 68 and send me somewhere quite far away, which was-- which was near [INAUDIBLE]. And I went to camp 50, which was quite-- quite a punishment. Me and my friend, two of us, wished to escape. So we had to start all over again. And, you know, we had already established some standing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some-- and it was. But it-- actually, we-- we [? were ?] were sort of freed on 18th of January in 1949. And I was the last one who left the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camp 50. You remained in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Camp 50, yes. 19--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when you left Israel, actually, Israel was really in a real existence in a very short time. I don't know, for some reason they-- they could not issue any-- any passport. But I needed passport.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came with this little traveling document, which was [INAUDIBLE]. And that was the one which was mine. It looked like a-- it acted like a passport. It wasn't a passport. I see. And that-- and how old were you at this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 23.

speaker: interviewer
text: Photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo, I think it was taken two years ago. It must have been some family celebration.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is on it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I'm starting from-- from the right, or from the left on the-- on the photograph, the eldest brother, Alexander Schmell-- Schmell [INAUDIBLE] Lowy. And this is her sister, Edith. That's the second one. And the third one, it's me, this one. And on the very end, it's Frank.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you standing in the order of your age?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is why I sort of picked this one.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's good to identify.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good? These are my two daughters. On the left-- left of me is Helen. She was born in 1966 in November. And on my right is Rebecca. She was born in 1973 in March.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The photo was taken about a year ago. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thanks. OK. John, would you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Helen Rachel Lowy, she's my first daughter born in 1966, November the 1st.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And thank you very much for coming. I'm so glad that you are here. Because this-- I think this will be a historical document, if you call, or whatever. It involves all of us because-- because you're a part of me and I am part of you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen, have you ever heard your father's story before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, lots of times since I was a little girl, all sorts of different shapes and forms, snippets along the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever heard it in a sequence?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I guess different situations or questions have arisen over the years. And so there's always been an answer, but not a chronological order. No, not really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have-- has he ever told you his stories of survival?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but he's very modest about it and doesn't really talk about it unless he's prompted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, he-- he ascribes his survival to luck or fate. Do you want to say something about that, John?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Sorry. You'll have to tell me. I wasn't here.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, you put-- you-- you ascribe your survival to fate, or good luck, or a miracle.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, I think it's destiny. Every human being has got a destiny. Every human being has got an opportunity to do things. It's only the timing that's the important part, when, and why, and for what. And-- and the strength or the desire to live and to survive was very, very strong. And I think that by surviving, or why I really survived-- first of all, I was sure that I'm going to. Never went a moment to mind that I'm not going to survive it. I approached everything in a sort of definite and positive way. I was never afraid. I was always naturally positioned. So that already gave me a much better-- I could plan my survival much better. And I gave less chance to other people to recognize my weakness.

speaker: interviewer
text: I gather from the tape that when you worked with people you endeared yourself to them. Do you think that was one of the reasons for your survival other then your goals and your outlook?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was very much part of my survival. You see, I have a sort of a enthusiasm when I go to things. And enthusiasm is quite a strong energy. And when I work with people or when I meet people, I always see in the people the best. And I show my best. And it works. Not always. I mean, I was disappointed in my life. But I think I take that risk. I think for every one beautiful and good. I [INAUDIBLE] a chance. Make something out of it, and [INAUDIBLE]. And this-- this was. It's-- it's-- sort of if there were ever-- I think-- when I'm going back to my childhood, and it wasn't my survival at stake, just socially, I have such a lot of friends, not necessarily Jewish, [? as ?] [? well as ?] Gentile. Because I treat every human being for what they are. I accept everybody. I think possibly that helped, had a lot to do with how I arranged my sort of cat and mouse game [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have anything to comment of being-- about being child of a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess not only having my father as a survivor, but my grandmother, and other members of the family, it gives you a different perspective sometimes on life. It certainly-- every child has criticisms about their family and their parents. And I think sometimes you are a little bit more forgiving when you think about the terrible things that they would have experienced and how they managed to overcome them. You're a little softer on them. It also makes me very proud to be here today, to be able to say that, you know, my family came through it. And we've had a wonderful life. Australia has been a fantastic country to my family. I've had a wonderful childhood. And I owe that to my parents and to their-- their ability to put the past behind them and get on with things. And I think that says a lot to the Jewish community all over the world. And I think that's something to be really proud of. It's a good foundation to start our own lives on now, our children and our children's children.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that beautifully. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: I also would like to add something. Talking about Holocaust, I mean, the Holocaust, it's all connected [? to this ?] thought that beating, hunger, torture. My situation is very unique. During all those Holocaust years, I never be touched. I never been spoken harshly on me. [INAUDIBLE] I never had hunger. I never had anything. I lived in a physical way good as any other time. Obviously, emotionally and mentally it was a big struggle. But I didn't feel any physical discomfort.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you saw the hunger. And you saw the suffering, didn't you? OK. Thank you very much. It was wonderful to interview you. I think I have also gained from it. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
